content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: Given a long URL, return a shorter alias. Given a short alias, redirect to the original URL. Links may expire.
Non-Functional: Highly available, low latency for reads, high scale (billions of URLs).

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. API Gateway/Load Balancer to route traffic.
2. Web servers to handle generation and redirection.
3. Database (NoSQL like Cassandra or DynamoDB is preferred for scale and high writes/reads).
4. Key Generation Service (KGS) to pre-generate unique IDs.

content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: 1-on-1 chat, group chat, message status (sent, delivered, read).
Non-Functional: Low latency, high availability, strong ordering.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. Use WebSockets for persistent, bidirectional connections.
2. Chat servers to hold WebSocket connections.
3. Message queue (Kafka) for asynchronous processing and persistence.
4. Database for message storage (Cassandra/HBase).

content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: Upload videos, view videos, search.
Non-Functional: High availability, low latency streaming, large storage capacity.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. Metadata DB (MySQL/PostgreSQL) and Object Storage (S3) for videos.
2. Transcoding pipeline (message queue + workers) to convert videos into multiple formats/resolutions.
3. CDN (Content Delivery Network) to serve video chunks globally with low latency.

content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: Drivers send location updates, riders request rides, matching system.
Non-Functional: Low latency, high availability, geospatial querying.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. Location Service to receive high-frequency driver updates.
2. Geospatial Database (Redis with Geospatial features or specialized DB like QuadTree index).
3. Matching Engine to pair riders and drivers.

content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: Post tweets, follow users, view home feed.
Non-Functional: High availability, fast feed generation.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. Feed Generation: Fan-out on write (push model) vs Fan-out on read (pull model).
2. Use hybrid approach: Push for inactive users, Pull for celebrities.
3. Cache feeds aggressively (Redis).

content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: Product catalog, shopping cart, checkout, order management.
Non-Functional: High availability, strong consistency for inventory/payments.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. Microservices architecture (Catalog, Cart, Order, Payment).
2. Search indexing (Elasticsearch) for fast product discovery.
3. Distributed transactions or Sagas for checkout.

content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: Start from seed URLs, extract links, store content.
Non-Functional: High scale, politeness (don't DDoS servers), fault tolerance.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. URL Frontier to manage the queue of URLs to crawl.
2. DNS caching to speed up resolution.
3. HTML fetchers and parsers.
4. Content deduplication using checksums/hashes.

content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: Limit requests based on IP/User ID.
Non-Functional: Very low latency, minimal memory footprint.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. Use Redis as a fast, centralized store.
2. Implement Token Bucket or Sliding Window algorithm using Redis scripting (Lua) for atomicity.

content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: Key-value storage, eviction policies.
Non-Functional: Low latency, scalability, fault tolerance.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. Consistent Hashing to distribute keys across nodes while minimizing data movement during scaling.
2. LRU/LFU eviction policies.
3. Replication for high availability.

content.heading: Requirements
paragraphs: Functional: Send Push/SMS/Email notifications to users.
Non-Functional: High throughput, guaranteed delivery, track status.

content.heading: High Level Design
paragraphs: 1. Message queues (Kafka) to decouple services and buffer bursts.
2. Dedicated workers for Push, SMS, and Email.
3. Retry mechanisms and dead-letter queues for failures.
